title: Assignment ØKA201 Data Analysis
text:
Abstract
This paper investigates how reading and writing performance are associated with mathematics achievement among students. Using regression analysis in R, several econometric models are estimated and compared, including simple regression, multiple regression, dummy variable analysis, and non-linear specifications. The findings suggest that reading and writing scores are positively related to mathematics performance.
Keywords: regression analysis, R programming, student performance, mathematics, Quarto


Introduction
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
library(tidyverse)
library(OEKA201AssignmentARVID)
data <- data.frame(data)

model1 <- lm(math.score ~ reading.score, data=data)
summary(model1)

model2 <- lm(math.score ~ reading.score + writing.score, data=data)
summary(model2)

data$female <- ifelse(data$gender == "female", 1, 0)

model3 <- lm(math.score ~ reading.score + female, data=data)
summary(model3)

model4 <- lm(math.score ~ reading.score + I(reading.score^2), data=data)
summary(model4)



model1
```
The purpose of this paper is to analyze factors affecting student performance using regression analysis in R. The analysis focuses on the relationship between reading, writing, and mathematics scores using the Students Performance dataset from Kaggle.
The paper builds upon the previous Gretl assignment and replicates the econometric analysis in R. According to (wickham2017r?), R provides flexible tools for statistical computing, data wrangling, and visualization which are useful for empirical economic analysis.


1. Research Question
The research question is:
How are reading and writing scores associated with mathematics performance among students?


2. Data Set
The dataset used in this analysis is the Students Performance dataset from Kaggle. The dataset contains information about gender, parental education, lunch type, test preparation course, and student exam scores in mathematics, reading, and writing.


3. Data Description and Summary
The dataset contains 1000 observations of student performance. The average mathematics score is approximately 66, while the average reading score is approximately 69.


4. Scatterplot Analysis
The scatterplot suggests a positive relationship between reading scores and mathematics scores.


5. Simple Regression Model
The estimated coefficient for reading score is positive and statistically significant at the 5 percent significance level. This indicates that higher reading performance is associated with higher mathematics scores.


6. Multiple Regression Model
The multiple regression model includes both reading and writing scores as explanatory variables. Both variables contribute positively to mathematics performance.


7. Dummy Variable Analysis
The dummy variable captures differences between male and female students. The coefficient for the female variable is statistically significant, indicating systematic differences between groups.


8. Non-linear Effects
The non-linear model includes a quadratic term for reading score. The quadratic term is not strongly significant, suggesting that the relationship is approximately linear.


9. Model Comparison
The multiple regression model provides the best balance of explanatory power and theoretical relevance.


Conclusion
The analysis indicates a positive relationship between reading performance and mathematics performance. Reading and writing skills are important predictors of student outcomes in mathematics. The multiple regression model provided the most useful explanation of student mathematics performance in this dataset.

library(OEKA201AssignmentARVID)
cword("assignment_r.qmd")

[1] 682

```{r}
summary(model1)

model2 <- lm(math.score ~ reading.score + writing.score, data=data)
summary(model2)

data$female <- ifelse(data$gender == "female", 1, 0)

model3 <- lm(math.score ~ reading.score + female, data=data)
summary(model3)

model4 <- lm(math.score ~ reading.score + I(reading.score^2), data=data)
summary(model4)



```

title: Assignment ØKA201 Data Analysis
text:
2. Simple Regression Model



Call:
lm(formula = math.score ~ reading.score, data = data)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-24.3419  -6.3419  -0.0221   6.2713  24.6581 

Coefficients:
              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)    7.35759    1.33818   5.498 4.87e-08 ***
reading.score  0.84910    0.01893  44.855  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 8.736 on 998 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6684,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6681 
F-statistic:  2012 on 1 and 998 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16


The estimated coefficient for reading score is positive and statistically significant at the 5% significance level. This indicates that higher reading performance is associated with higher mathematics scores.
The simple regression model explains a large proportion of the variation in mathematics scores.

title: Assignment ØKA201 Data Analysis
text:
3. Multiple Regression Model



Call:
lm(formula = math.score ~ reading.score + writing.score, data = data)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-23.8779  -6.1750   0.2693   6.0184  24.8727 

Coefficients:
              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)    7.52409    1.32823   5.665 1.93e-08 ***
reading.score  0.60129    0.06304   9.538  < 2e-16 ***
writing.score  0.24942    0.06057   4.118 4.14e-05 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 8.667 on 997 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.674, Adjusted R-squared:  0.6733 
F-statistic:  1031 on 2 and 997 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16


The multiple regression model includes both reading and writing scores as explanatory variables. Both variables contribute positively to mathematics performance.
The multiple regression model provides a better explanation of variation in mathematics scores compared to the simple regression model.

title: Assignment ØKA201 Data Analysis
text:
4-1. Dummy Variable Analysis



Call:
lm(formula = math.score ~ reading.score + female, data = data)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-22.7447  -4.3918  -0.0747   4.1590  18.1338 

Coefficients:
              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)     6.6384     1.0078   6.587 7.26e-11 ***
reading.score   0.9483     0.0147  64.523  < 2e-16 ***
female        -11.8614     0.4292 -27.634  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 6.577 on 997 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.8122,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.8119 
F-statistic:  2157 on 2 and 997 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16


The dummy variable captures differences between male and female students. The coefficient estimates whether female students perform differently in mathematics after controlling for reading performance.
The coefficient for the female variable is statistically significant, indicating systematic differences between groups.

title: Assignment ØKA201 Data Analysis
text:
4-2. Non-linear Effects



Call:
lm(formula = math.score ~ reading.score + I(reading.score^2), 
    data = data)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-24.4463  -6.3301   0.0727   6.2636  24.5537 

Coefficients:
                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)         4.9091162  4.3148582   1.138    0.256    
reading.score       0.9255394  0.1294523   7.150 1.68e-12 ***
I(reading.score^2) -0.0005681  0.0009517  -0.597    0.551    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 8.738 on 997 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6686,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6679 
F-statistic:  1006 on 2 and 997 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16


The non-linear model includes a quadratic term for reading score in order to test whether the relationship between reading and mathematics performance changes at different score levels.
The quadratic term is not strongly significant, suggesting that the relationship is approximately linear.

title: Assignment ØKA201 Data Analysis
text:
5. Model Selection and Analysis
The different models were compared based on explanatory power and theoretical relevance. The multiple regression and non-linear models provide improved model fit compared to the simple regression model.
Based on the results, the preferred model is the multiple regression model because it captures more information relevant to mathematics performance.
The preferred model can be used to predict mathematics scores for students with different reading and writing performance levels.

title: OEKA201AssignmentKLBRHBAY
text:
Weaknesses

Overly generic and thin empirical discussion

The descriptions of results are very general (e.g., “positive and statistically significant”) without:

Reporting actual coefficient estimates, standard errors, t‑statistics, or confidence intervals.
Quantifying economic/educational significance (e.g., “a 10‑point increase in reading is associated with X points in math”).

No discussion of goodness‑of‑fit beyond a brief statement about R‑squared.

Code is largely commented out or incomplete

Many critical R commands are commented out (#model1 <- ..., #plot(...), #summary(data), etc.). If run as‑is, the document will not produce the claimed analyses or figures.
In the Introduction and Appendix, the {{< include "script/estm.R" >}} syntax is used inside an R chunk, which mixes Quarto shortcodes with knitr syntax in a way that may not work as intended.
The dataset loading step is missing in the visible code (data <- ...) so the analysis is not reproducible from the document alone.

Limited data description and exploration

The “Data Description and Summary” section only states the number of observations and rough means for two variables.
There is no:

Full summary table or descriptive statistics (min, max, sd).
Table of variables with definitions and units.
Missing values check or data cleaning discussion.
Consideration of the categorical variables (parental education, lunch, test prep, gender) beyond a single dummy for female.


Underdeveloped diagnostic and robustness analysis

No regression diagnostics are reported:

Residual plots, heteroskedasticity checks, normality of residuals, influential observations, or multicollinearity (especially between reading and writing).

The non‑linear specification is only briefly mentioned, without graphical evidence (e.g., scatterplot with fitted curve) or more flexible forms (e.g., logs, splines).
Model comparison is based solely on R‑squared; no adjusted R‑squared, AIC/BIC, or cross‑validation are discussed.

Weak treatment of econometric issues

The assignment does not discuss:

Potential omitted variable bias (e.g., socio‑economic status, school quality, test prep).
Endogeneity concerns or causal interpretation (the results are described as associations, but this caveat could be made more explicit).
Interpretation of dummy variable coefficient (e.g., “female” shifts in intercept, holding reading constant).

Significance is mentioned but the choice of significance level, multiple testing, and practical vs. statistical significance are not elaborated.

Superficial presentation of visualizations

The scatterplot chunk is commented out, so no figure is actually produced.
The text description of the scatterplot is very brief and does not:

Comment on dispersion, potential outliers, or non‑linearity.
Include any regression line or confidence band.

No additional plots (histograms, boxplots by gender, etc.) are used to understand the data.

Quarto/LaTeX structure and citation issues

There are formatting irregularities:

Multiple colons (:) at line ends that look like artifacts from copying; they may cause rendering issues.
Code fences like {r} instead of standard Quarto{r} (unless there is a specific template, this is likely erroneous).

The reference to @wickham2017r is not supported by a visible bibliography file or proper bibliography: field in the YAML; reproducible references may not work.
The # References section is an empty div (::: {#refs}) without a linked .bib file.

Conclusions are not fully justified by presented evidence

The conclusion restates that reading/writing “are important predictors” but:

Does not reference magnitudes, diagnostics, or robustness checks.
Does not mention any limitations or potential alternative explanations.

Practical or policy implications (if any) are not discussed, even in a brief, cautious way.

Reproducibility and transparency

Reliance on script/estm.R without showing its core content in the main text makes it hard for a reader to understand or reproduce the analysis.
The name of the dataset file and its loading code are not shown, so an independent reader cannot rerun the assignment from scratch.

title: OEKA201AssignmentKLBRHBAY
text:
Summary
In its current form, the assignment has a well‑defined question, a sensible econometric structure, and a good overall layout in Quarto. However, the empirical content is underdeveloped: key code is commented out, numerical results are not reported, diagnostics are missing, and the treatment of data and econometric issues is quite superficial. Strengthening the implementation (uncommenting and verifying code, adding proper summaries and tables) and deepening the interpretation (more detailed results, diagnostics, and discussion of limitations) would significantly improve the quality of the assignment.
